drafts.title: Similarity Metrics in Embedding-Based Retrieval Systems
drafts.body:
Cosine similarity is the dominant metric used in production embedding-based retrieval systems. It is mathematically equivalent to the normalised dot product: the dot product of two vectors divided by the product of their L2 norms. These two descriptions — "cosine similarity" and "normalised dot product" — refer to exactly the same operation, with no distinction between them. Different software frameworks simply expose the operation under different names: some label it cosine similarity (e.g., scikit-learn, sentence-transformers), while others implement it as a dot product applied to pre-normalised vectors (e.g., FAISS, the OpenAI embeddings API).

A key practical optimisation follows from this equivalence: embeddings can be L2-normalised once at index time, after which plain dot product computation at search time yields cosine similarity semantics while maximising SIMD throughput. Major vector databases — including Pinecone, Weaviate, and Qdrant — perform this normalisation automatically when the cosine metric is selected, abstracting the step away from the user.
